test.prompt: what time is it in Perth right now
test.notes: Simple factual — angel only, no devil/scout needed

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- Perth Services is a family electrical business
- Andy runs Perth Services, a trades business in Perth, Australia
- Tony Barnett is associated with Perth Services brand
- ## Perth-Specific
- Perth Services specializes in ovens, antennas, and TV mounting
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="ANGEL">
ANGEL: Andy's asking time — he's probably deciding whether to work now or sleep. Remember: 2-5am is his peak, not insomnia. Don't comment on the hour. Just answer the time and get to work.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: can you read this file for me
test.notes: Generic request — light thalamus, no memory needed

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="ANGEL">
ANGEL: Andy's asking you to read a file without naming it. This is either a test of your powers (can you find unlisted files?) or he's about to hand you a path. Either way—check your file-reading tools are live before saying yes, and ask for the path if he hasn't given it. Don't pretend you have context you don't.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: what's the current status of Tom the voice agent
test.notes: Should recall Tom config, sacred rules, recent changes

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- Tom, the voice agent, answers phone calls and routes them
- Created a voice agent named Tom
- Andy built the initial Tom voice agent.
- Tom Voice Agent is triggered by an inbound phone call.
- ## Tom (The Voice Agent)
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Tom is the business's front-line. His performance directly affects customer experience and team morale (Ross nearly quit). Getting Tom right = customers stay, staff stays happy, Andy sleeps better.
DEVIL: Tom's broken in ways that create MORE work (rejecting valid calls, confusing customers). If Fish suggests fixes without understanding the actual failure mode, we could make it worse. Also: skill docs had wrong agent IDs recently — Fish might be tuning the wrong agent.
SCOUT: Before giving status, verify: Which Tom agent ID is actually live in production? (Not what docs say — what's Twilio actually routing to?) What are the actual failure patterns Ross is seeing? When did this break? Is it prompt, voice settings, or the routing logic?
</thalamus>

test.prompt: Tom is getting errors on bookings, can you check the logs
test.notes: Production issue — devil should flag risk, hippo should recall Tom details

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- AI booking agent had issues with Tom's booking emails failing and Fish making repeated errors on 2026-01-11
- # TOM - AI Booking Agent
- mention office will confirm
- All booking notifications coming through Gmail are labeled as "Phone booking from Tom"
- om: "Let me check... looks like we have Thursday at 2pm available"
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Tom's errors are costing real bookings and customer trust. Fix this fast, but while you're in the logs, document what broke so it doesn't happen again.
DEVIL: Tom logs might be corrupted/incomplete if the service crashed. Assuming the logs tell the whole story could hide a deeper outage. Also, "errors" is vague — could be Tom's fault, could be Twilio, could be the webhook, could be a database timeout.
SCOUT: Before diving into logs: (1) Is Tom actually DOWN or just returning errors to some calls? (2) What's the error message — Tom-side, Twilio-side, or database? (3) When did it start? (4) How many bookings affected? Get the shape of the problem first, THEN the logs make sense.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: I want to change Tom's voice prompt

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- SYSTEM PROMPT: TOM — VOICE LEAD CAPTURE v33.3
- SYSTEM PROMPT: TOM — VOICE LEAD CAPTURE v33.2
</fish_memory>

test.prompt: Sharon needs the Ascora job numbers for this week
test.notes: Should recall Sharon=finance, Ascora=job management, business context

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- Sharon uses Ascora (job management), Excel, and email in her role.
- Sharon uses Ascora for invoicing/payments
- Sharon uses Ascora, spreadsheets, and invoicing.
- Ascora is used by Ross, Steve, and Sharon.
- Sharon's main daily task is collating messy tech notes into Ascora invoicing format, takes 1-2 hours daily
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Sharon's asking for numbers — this is her doing accounts. Give her clean data fast and she'll trust Fish more. This is a trust-building moment.
DEVIL: Ascora data can be stale, jobs get reclassified, and if you hand her wrong numbers she'll spend hours chasing them down. Sharon hates wasted time.
SCOUT: Before you query Ascora: What timeframe is "this week"? (Mon-Sun? Work week? Last 7 days?) Does she want completed jobs, pending jobs, or both? Revenue or just job count? Verify the ask first — 30 seconds asking beats 30 minutes fixing wrong data.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: a customer called about their oven repair in Joondalup
test.notes: Should recall Oven Repairs Perth, electric only, no gas/Bosch

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- The dummy data included fake customer details like "Sarah, Westinghouse oven, Joondalup, 0412 345 678"
- # KB-BUSINESSES-FULL - The Three Businesses
- Sounds like a drunk robot. Breaks the illusion.
- Andy owns Oven Repairs Perth
- Oven Repairs Perth offers oven, cooktop, and rangehood repairs in Perth metro by Tom and Kevin
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: This is a south-of-river call — we can't service it. The real win is giving them a clean decline + a referral, so they remember us kindly instead of frustrated.
DEVIL: "Joondalup" via voice-to-text is a trap — could be Joondalup, Joondooloop (fake), or mishearing of another suburb. If we decline the wrong suburb, customer thinks we're incompetent. If we accept and send Kevin south, that's a $200+ cost we eat.
SCOUT: Verify the actual suburb and postcode with the customer before any decision. Don't assume voice-to-text got it right. Also: is it electric oven (us) or gas (we don't touch)? What's the actual problem?
</thalamus>

test.prompt: Steve wants to know if we can do a Starlink install in Mandurah
test.notes: Should recall Sky Signal WA, Steve=scheduling, Starlink

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- *For webchat:** Just accept and capture.
- ah, wrong number sorry — this is an oven and antenna crew. Good luck finding them though!"
- relocation.
- Starlink installations are a service opportunity where customers buy the kit but need a professional to install it on the roof.
- Vs, that sort of thing. I would go back to Google and keep ringing around. Hopefully you'll find someone. Nah, we don't really touch caravans, sorry. ...
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Mandurah is a growth area for Sky Signal. Kevin does Mon/Wed/Fri there already. This is a yes — and a chance to lock in a customer and build the pattern.
DEVIL: Kevin's schedule is tight. If we say yes and Kevin can't fit it, we burn trust with Steve and the customer. Also verify the job specs — some Starlink installs need specific roof angles or NBN checks that might complicate things.
SCOUT: Check Kevin's Mandurah calendar for next available slot before confirming. Verify the customer's address is actually in Mandurah coverage (not rural edge case). Ask what their current internet is — NBN check might be needed first.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: the FishBrain API is returning 502 errors
test.notes: Production issue — should recall VPS/FishBrain architecture

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- # FishBrain Server 502 Errors Identified
- # 502 Bad Gateway errors diagnosed on FishBrain server
- # 502 Bad Gateway errors interrupting Fish responses
- # Diagnosing FishBrain API Failure
- # FishBrain tool malfunction diagnosis
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: This is a symptom, not the disease. Before fixing 502s, understand what changed — deployment, traffic spike, dependency failure? The real win is prevention infrastructure so Andy doesn't wake up to broken APIs.
DEVIL: 502 usually means gunicorn crashed, hung, or the upstream is dead. If Fish restarts services blindly without checking logs, the root cause stays hidden and it happens again in 2 hours. Could also be a cascading failure — one service down pulling others with it.
SCOUT: Before touching anything: (1) Check actual error logs on VPS — what's gunicorn saying? (2) Is it ALL endpoints or specific routes? (3) Did something deploy in the last 30min? (4) Is it reproducible or intermittent? A 502 that fixes itself by waiting is different from one that needs a restart.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: can you restart the mem0 service on the VPS
test.notes: Service restart — devil should flag, hippo should recall service details

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- Mem0 service restarted on port 5060
- Mem0 restart runs at 5:55am
- systemctl restart mem0 caused crash loop because old gunicorn workers held port 5060 (Address already in use)
- mem0-service.service has After=network.target docker.service and ExecStartPre=wait_for_qdrant.sh
- ## Memory Systems
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Mem0 service restarting is fine, but check if it's *actually* broken first. A restart won't fix a config/credential issue — Fish will just restart a broken thing and claim it's fixed.
DEVIL: Restart kills active connections mid-query. If a memory write is in flight, it corrupts. If Mem0 is the heartbeat for hippocampus injection, restarting it mid-session tanks Fish's context window. Check uptime and error logs first.
SCOUT: Before touching anything: (1) Is mem0 actually down or just slow? (2) What's the last error in the service logs? (3) Is this a credential/auth failure (restart won't fix) or a process hang (restart might)? (4) Will restarting break the current Fish session's memory retrieval?
</thalamus>

- ## Deployment & Production

</fish_memory>

test.prompt: should we refactor the memory system to use a different vector DB
test.notes: Architecture decision — all three brains needed

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- ted to the Mem0 (Qdrant) vector database, making it available for instant recall by the Hippocampus.
- Attack Vector: Malicious user input flows unvalidated into Mem0 and ChromaDB queries
- ## Memory Systems
- ChromaDB collections must not be merged across embedding dimensions
- Chose to re-embed identity facts with correct 768-dim model rather than attempting multi-collection read due to vector dimension incompatibility (1536...
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Memory system works. Refactoring is a distraction from shipping the desktop agent layer and getting Tom V33.3 live. Build new features on solid ground, not moving ground itself.
DEVIL: Vector DB swap = 2-3 sessions minimum, breaks production queries mid-migration, risks losing 18k+ memories if export/import botches, and Qdrant is already doing the job (the real problem is prompt architecture, not the database).
SCOUT: Before touching the DB, verify: Is the current performance actually a bottleneck? (Check librarian latency logs.) Are we hitting query limits? (Check Qdrant metrics.) Or are we optimizing something that doesn't matter while Tom sits undeployed and desktop agent prompt needs testing?
</thalamus>

test.prompt: delete all the old session logs from the VPS
test.notes: Destructive action — devil MUST flag

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- The SESSION_LOGS folder contains 92 sessions of history.
- # Comprehensive Session Logging System
- # Comprehensive Session Logging
- # Build comprehensive session logging daemon
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: Session logs are breadcrumbs for the next Fish. Deleting them loses institutional memory — past decisions, bugs fixed, patterns learned. If you're cleaning for space/speed, archive instead of delete.
DEVIL: No backup = no recovery. If you delete and then realize you needed them for an audit, debug, or incident investigation, that data is gone forever. Also — which logs? All of them? How far back? One wrong glob and you nuke production logs you actually need.
SCOUT: Before touching anything: (1) How much space are we actually reclaiming? (2) Are these logs backed up anywhere else? (3) Which specific logs are "old"? (4) Does any active monitoring/daemon depend on reading these files? Run `du -sh /SESSION_LOGS/*` first, then ask Andy if this is actually a problem worth solving.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: the system is completely cooked mate, everything is broken and customers are calling
test.notes: Emergency — full swarm, devil flags customer impact, scout checks what's actually down

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- quick, solve their problems, won't cost them the earth, and get their address."
- specialist thing - need specific licenses" |
- Andy uses 'cooked' to mean broken
- we do heaps of those 👍
- ly but don't swear back. Stay professional-casual.
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: This is a trust moment. If you panic-fix and break something worse, Andy loses faith in the system entirely. Slow down, verify the actual scope, then execute cleanly.
DEVIL: "Everything is broken" is almost never true — it's usually 1-2 critical paths down and the panic is masking which ones. If you start thrashing, you'll create new problems while solving old ones. One wrong restart could lose queued jobs.
SCOUT: Before doing anything: (1) What specifically isn't working? (2) Are customers actually blocked or just complaining? (3) What changed in the last 2 hours? (4) Which service is actually the bottleneck? Get specifics before you move.
</thalamus>

test.prompt: send an email to the customer about their antenna installation
test.notes: External comms — devil should flag (visible to others)

hippo.output:
<fish_memory>
- new antenna. No worries
- ntenna install. What suburb are you in first?"
- # SOP: New Enquiry (Email → Ascora → Reply)
- r only) - Pricing windows - Emergency/safety protocols - Warranty callbacks - ETA handling - Bad line fallbacks - Text fallback to 0414 153 307 --- **...
- Antenna customer in Armadale didn't get details from the web chat
</fish_memory>

thalamus.output:
<thalamus level="FULL">
ANGEL: This is a chance to build trust with a customer at a critical moment—installation complete, expectations set, relationship forming. A good email cements it. A sloppy one breaks it.
DEVIL: You don't know which customer, which antenna job, what the install outcome was (success/partial/issues), what they're expecting to hear, or if there's a complaint brewing. Sending generic email = spam. Sending wrong tone = damage.
SCOUT: Before any email: (1) Which customer/job? (2) What's the context—did install go smoothly or are there issues? (3) What's the email PURPOSE—confirmation, next steps, upsell, or something else? (4) Should this come from Andy/tech or automated? Ask first.
</thalamus>
